request.question: What artist created a traditional folk song among prisoners in the American South as well as Maybellene?

title: Sarie Marais
Here We à Go Go Again!
Contemporary folk music
On Top of Old Smoky
The House of the Rising Sun
Song So-hee
Folk Den
Billy Boy
Midnight Special (song)
Min'yō
sentences: "Sarie Marais" (also known as "My Sarie Marais" and pronounced "May SAH-ree mah-REH") is a traditional South African folk song, created during either the First Anglo-Boer War (c. 1880) (less likely) or the Second Anglo-Boer War (ca. 1900).

The tune was possibly taken from a song dating from the American Civil War called "Ellie Rhee" (itself perhaps a version of the traditional folk song "Foggy Dew"), with the words translated into Afrikaans.
Here We à Go Go Again!
is Johnny Rivers's second official album, and like his first album, "At the Whisky à Go Go", it was recorded live at the Whisky a Go Go in Los Angeles, California.
The album reached #38 on the Billboard Charts and spawned two hit singles: "Maybellene" and "Midnight Special".
Contemporary folk music refers to a wide variety of genres that emerged in the mid 20th century and afterwards which were associated with traditional folk music.
Starting in the mid-20th century a new form of popular folk music evolved from traditional folk music.
This process and period is called the (second) folk revival and reached a zenith in the 1960s.
The most common name for this new form of music is also "folk music", but is often called "contemporary folk music" or "folk revival music" to make the distinction.
The transition was somewhat centered in the US and is also called the American folk music revival.
Fusion genres such as folk rock, folktronica, and others also evolved within this phenomenon.

While contemporary folk music is a genre generally distinct from traditional folk music, it often shares the same English name, performers and venues as traditional folk music; even individual songs may be a blend of the two.
"On Top of Old Smokey" is a traditional folk song of the United States.
As recorded by The Weavers, the song reached the pop music charts in 1951.
It is catalogued as Roud Folk Song Index No. 414.
In one version the first verse is the following; for more on the words see below.
"The House of the Rising Sun" is a traditional folk song, sometimes called "Rising Sun Blues".
It tells of a life gone wrong in New Orleans; many versions also urge a sibling to avoid the same fate.
The most successful commercial version, recorded in 1964 by British rock group the Animals, was a number one hit on the UK Singles Chart and also in the United States and France.
As a traditional folk song recorded by an electric rock band, it has been described as the "first folk-rock hit".
Song So-hee (born October 20, 1997), is a South Korean singer who specializes in minyo, traditional Korean folk songs.
She has been active since childhood and has often been called a "young female master singer".
She became famous nationwide after she performed "Changbu Taryeong", a well-known Korean traditional folk song, at the KBS National Singing Contest.
She left a deep impression upon the viewers with her performance.
Since then she has been invited to perform at various events, both at home and abroad.
Currently she is a first-year college student at Dankook University (as a traditional music major) and successfully manages her life as a student and a performer.
Folk Den is a folk music website founded in 1995 by Roger McGuinn, former front man of The Byrds.
Hosted at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill's ibiblio, the site is intended to preserve and promote folk music and offers a new folk song on a monthly basis.
Each posting provides an MP3 of a traditional folk song along with a descriptive paragraph, lyrics, guitar chords and related images.
The site has received positive reviews from "The New York Times", the Discovery Channel, and CNET.
"Billy Boy" is a traditional folk song and nursery rhyme found in the United States.
It has a Roud Folk Song Index number of 326.
It is a variant of the traditional English folk song "My Boy Billy", collected by Ralph Vaughan Williams and published by him in 1912 as number 232 in "Novello's School Songs".
The song is very popular with the Orange Order.
"Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South.
The title comes from the refrain, which refers to the passenger train "Midnight Special" and its "ever-loving light" (sometimes "ever-living light"):
Min'yō (民謡 ) is a genre of traditional Japanese music.
The term is a translation of the German word "Volkslied" (folk song) and has only been in use since the twentieth century.

Japanese traditional designations referring to more or less the same genre include "inaka bushi" ("country song") "inaka buri" ("country tune"), "hina uta" ("rural song") and the like, but for most of the people who sang such songs they were simply "uta" (song).

The term min'yō is now sometimes also used to refer to traditional songs of other countries, though a preceding adjective is needed: Furansu min'yō = French folk song; for this reason, many sources in Japanese also feel the need to preface the term with "Nihon": Nihon min'yō = Japanese [traditional] folk song.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What artist created a traditional folk song among prisoners in the American South as well as Maybellene?
hop1_docs: Midnight Special (song) | "Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South.  The title comes from the refrain, which refers to the passenger train "Midnight Special" and its "ever-loving light" (sometimes "ever-living light"):

Song Chang-sik | Song Chang-Sik (born 2 February, 1947) is a South Korean folk singer-songwriter.  He is well known for denying the traditional Solfège (both Korean and Western), and re-interpreting the melody on his own.  These melodies can be hardly written with traditional note system, as he "creates" notes that never existed.b

Sarie Marais | "Sarie Marais" (also known as "My Sarie Marais" and pronounced "May SAH-ree mah-REH") is a traditional South African folk song, created during either the First Anglo-Boer War (c. 1880) (less likely) or the Second Anglo-Boer War (ca. 1900).  The tune was possibly taken from a song dating from the American Civil War called "Ellie Rhee" (itself perhaps a version of the traditional folk song "Foggy Dew"), with the words translated into Afrikaans.

The Hollow Trees | The Hollow Trees play what they call “folk music for Families.”  It’s high energy acoustic Americana music played with passion, skill, and humor.  They combine the musical styles of bluegrass music, country music, folk music, blues and jazz and put their stamp on many traditional folk songs as well as original compositions.

Folsom Prison Blues | "Folsom Prison Blues" is a song written in 1953 and first recorded in 1955 by American singer-songwriter Johnny Cash.  The song combines elements from two popular folk styles, the train song and the prison song, both of which Cash continued to use for the rest of his career.  It was one of Cash's signature songs.  It was the eleventh track on his debut album "With His Hot and Blue Guitar" and it was also included (same version) on "All Aboard the Blue Train".  A live version, recorded among inmates at Folsom State Prison itself, became a #1 hit on the country music charts in 1968.

Down in the Valley (folk song) | "Down in the Valley", also known as "Birmingham Jail", is a traditional country-blues American folk song.  It has been recorded by many artists, and is included in the "Songs of Expanding America" recordings in the Burl Ives six-album set "Historical America in Song".

You Are My Little Bird | You Are My Little Bird is an album by Elizabeth Mitchell, released in 2006 by Smithsonian Folkways Recordings.  The album is a collection of children's music played in a folk music style.  In addition to versions of folk tunes, it features covers of a variety of songs by other artists, among them "What Goes On" by The Velvet Underground, Neil Young's "Little Wing", and "Three Little Birds" by Bob Marley.  On most of the songs, Mitchell is accompanied by family members.  The liner notes include personal stories from the artist, but no lyrics.
response.summary_1: Evidence: Passage 1 — "Midnight Special... is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South." Missing/bridge: the passages do not mention "Maybellene" or any artist who created/recorded both songs. Next-hop retrieval directions: (A) find the artist who "created" or is credited with the song "Maybellene" (search query: "Maybellene who wrote Maybellene artist"), and (B) check that artist's credits/discography for any creation or recording of "Midnight Special" (search query: "[artist name] Midnight Special discography" or "[artist name] recorded 'Midnight Special'"). Use the quoted phrase above as the anchor for the "Midnight Special" origin.
hop2_docs: Maybellene | "Maybellene" is one of the first rock and roll songs.  It was written and recorded in 1955 by Chuck Berry, and inspired/adapted from the Western Swing fiddle tune "Ida Red," which was recorded in 1938 by Bob Wills and his Texas Playboys.  Berry's song tells the story of a hot rod race and a broken romance.  It was released in July 1955 as a single by Chess Records, of Chicago, Illinois.  It was Berry's first single and his first hit.  "Maybellene" is considered one of the pioneering rock songs: "Rolling Stone" magazine wrote, "Rock & roll guitar starts here."  The record is an early instance of the complete rock-and-roll package: youthful subject matter; a small, guitar-driven combo; clear diction; and an atmosphere of unrelenting excitement.  The lyrics describe a man driving a V8 Ford chasing his unfaithful girlfriend in her Cadillac Coupe DeVille.

Chuck Berry House | The Chuck Berry House is the former home of Chuck Berry in St. Louis, Missouri located at 3137 Whittier Street.  The house was Berry's home when he wrote and first performed the majority of songs with which he is identified, including "Maybellene" (1955), "Roll Over Beethoven" (1956), "Too Much Monkey Business" (1956), "Rock and Roll Music" (1957), "School Day" (1957), "Sweet Little Sixteen" (1958), and "Johnny B. Goode" (1958).

Wee Wee Hours | "Wee Wee Hours" is a song composed and recorded by Chuck Berry in 1955.  Originally released as the B-side of his first single, "Maybellene", it went on to become a hit, reaching number 10 in the "Billboard" R&B chart.

Ida Red | "Ida Red" is an American traditional song of unknown origins that was made famous in the upbeat 1938 version by Bob Wills and his Texas Playboys.  Wills' Ida Red served as the primary inspiration for Chuck Berry's first big hit Maybellene.  It is chiefly identified by variations of the chorus:

Here We à Go Go Again! | Here We à Go Go Again!  is Johnny Rivers's second official album, and like his first album, "At the Whisky à Go Go", it was recorded live at the Whisky a Go Go in Los Angeles, California.  The album reached #38 on the Billboard Charts and spawned two hit singles: "Maybellene" and "Midnight Special".

No Money Down (Chuck Berry song) | "No Money Down" is a song written and recorded by Chuck Berry in December 1955.  The recording session was organized by Chess Records following the success of "Maybellene" and "Wee Wee Hours" singles the same year.  "No Money Down" was first released as a single in January 1956, with "Down Bound Train" on the B-side, reaching number 8 in the "Billboard" R&B chart.  The song was later included into Chuck Berry's 1957 album "After School Session".

Chuck Berry | Charles Edward Anderson Berry (October 18, 1926 – March 18, 2017) was an American singer, songwriter, musician, and one of the pioneers of rock and roll music.  With songs such as "Maybellene" (1955), "Roll Over Beethoven" (1956), "Rock and Roll Music" (1957) and "Johnny B. Goode" (1958), Berry refined and developed rhythm and blues into the major elements that made rock and roll distinctive.  Writing lyrics that focused on teen life and consumerism, and developing a music style that included guitar solos and showmanship, Berry was a major influence on subsequent rock music.
hop2_titles: Here We à Go Go Again!
response.summary_2: "Midnight Special" is a traditional folk song thought to have originated among prisoners in the American South, and "Maybellene" was written and recorded in 1955 by Chuck Berry. No single artist is credited with creating both (the former is traditional), though Johnny Rivers recorded both "Maybellene" and "Midnight Special" on his album Here We à Go Go Again!.
response.answer: No single artist is credited with creating both
new_support_titles_hop2: Here We à Go Go Again!
